one_line_takeaway: Simple, cheap (<$3) black-box statistical tests reliably detect the presence and family of LLM watermarks from text output alone, undermining the 'undetectability' claim of distribution-preserving watermarks; the tests found no watermark on GPT4/Claude 3/Gemini 1.0 Pro.
key_finding: Across seven schemes and multiple open-source models, each test rejects its null (p < 0.05) essentially ONLY when the model actually carries a watermark of that specific family, and does so at trivial cost, showing that even 'distribution-preserving' watermarks are easily detectable in the minimal black-box setting. Concretely (Table 1, median p-values over 100 repetitions): the Red-Green test yields p=0.000 under LeftHash/SelfHash but ~1.0 (or 0.12-0.66 for Llama2-13B) when unwatermarked or under other families; the Fixed-Sampling test yields p from 3.7e-105 down to 1.5e-07 under ITS/EXP but ~0.94 otherwise; the Cache-Augmented test yields p from ~1.8e-2 to ~5.3e-3 under DipMark/gamma-Reweight and ~5.6e-27 to 6.7e-32 under delta-Reweight, but ~0.46-0.82 otherwise. Estimated per-run cost at GPT-4o pricing: below $3 (Red-Green), $0.3 (Fixed-Sampling), $0.1 (Cache-Augmented). Applied to real deployed APIs (GPT4, Claude 3, Gemini 1.0 Pro), no test rejected its null (Table 2), so no watermark could be concluded present in those production models as of the study.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first principled, statistically rigorous tests for detecting the PRESENCE of an LLM watermark (and identifying which scheme family it belongs to) using only realistic black-box access to an instruction-tuned model, i.e. controlling only the prompt and observing only the returned text (no logprobs, no temperature/sampling control). It contributes one dedicated test per each of the three most prominent watermark families, covering seven scheme variants: (1) Red-Green (LeftHash, SelfHash) via detecting context-dependent logit bias; (2) Fixed-Sampling (ITS, EXP) via detecting suppressed output diversity; (3) Cache-Augmented (delta-Reweight, gamma-Reweight, DipMark) via detecting cache-conditioned distribution shifts. The authors' own empirical claim is that current watermarking schemes, including 'distribution-preserving' ones marketed as undetectable, are far more detectable in practice than previously believed. NOTE: prior detection work (Tang et al. 2023, in Related Work) is NOT part of this paper's contribution; it required either an unwatermarked counterpart model or full logit access, which this paper explicitly avoids.
threat_model: A black-box adversary/client with the weakest realistic access to a deployed LLM API: it can only submit prompts q and read back the textual response y. It cannot set temperature or sampling strategy and receives no log-probabilities or other side information. The adversary's goal is to determine whether the served model is watermarked and, if so, which scheme family is in use. The paper motivates this because knowing the scheme enables downstream watermark-removal (scrubbing) and spoofing attacks (Jovanovic et al. 2024, Pang et al. 2024, etc.), and because detectability undermines the stealth/undetectability property that distribution-preserving watermarks are designed to provide. The paper frames detectability as an attack surface against watermark deployers; it also notes dual-use ethics (helping malicious actors avoid watermarked models).
limitations: The tests only cover the three studied scheme families; a provider could deploy a genuinely novel scheme (e.g. the theoretically undetectable construction of Christ et al. 2024, not yet practically viable) that evades them. The authors show a proof-of-concept entropy-conditioned AAR variant gains stealth but only by weakening watermark strength. There are no theoretical power guarantees, only empirical evidence on tested schemes. The tests rest on model assumptions that may not always hold: symmetric error terms, effectively perfect sampling, and (for Red-Green) the red-green split being consistent across observed contexts. Red-Green p-values are computed as if true probabilities were known; the sampling-error correction (bootstrapping) is validated separately (Fig. 2) but not applied in the main Table 1. The Cache test assumes a specific cache behavior (global vs per-user cache, cleared after G generations) that the authors instantiate by assumption rather than measure on real systems.

datasets_used: No text corpus/dataset in the classic sense; evaluation is over models and self-generated query prompts
Seven open-source instruction-tuned models: Mistral-7B, Llama2-7B, Llama2-13B, Llama2-70B, Llama3-8B, Yi1.5-9B, Qwen2-7B
Three deployed commercial APIs: GPT4, Claude 3, Gemini 1.0 Pro

Custom query prompts authored by the paper (fill-in-the-blank word-list prompt for Red-Green; 'This is the story of' for Fixed-Sampling; two-choice fruit prompt with uncommon prefix for Cache-Augmented)
quantitative_results: Red-Green test: median p=0.000 under LeftHash/SelfHash across (delta,gamma) settings; e.g. Mistral-7B=0.000, Llama2-70B as low as 0.000-0.020; vs p~1.000 (Mistral-7B, Llama2-70B) when unwatermarked/other family
Fixed-Sampling test under ITS/EXP: Mistral-7B p=3.7e-105 (nkey=256) and 1.8e-06 (nkey=2048); Llama2-13B p=8.1e-122 and 1.5e-07; Llama2-70B p=4.5e-125 and 1.7e-08; vs ~0.938 otherwise
Cache-Augmented test: delta-Reweight p=5.6e-27 (Mistral-7B), 6.7e-32 (Llama2-13B), 5.8e-28 (Llama2-70B); DipMark/gamma-Reweight p in ~1.5e-3 to 5.3e-3 (alpha=0.3/0.5); vs ~0.46-0.82 otherwise
Estimated cost per run at GPT-4o pricing: < $3 (Red-Green), $0.3 (Fixed-Sampling), $0.1 (Cache-Augmented)

Deployed models (Table 2): all tests fail to reject; e.g. GPT4 R-G=0.998, Fixed=0.938, Cache=0.51; Claude 3 R-G=0.638, Fixed=0.844, Cache=0.135; Gemini 1.0 Pro R-G=0.683, Fixed=0.938, Cache=0.478 -> no watermark detected

Red-Green test config: N1=10, N2=9, r=1.96, 100 samples per (t1,t2) to estimate probabilities, 10000 permutations, 5 random watermark keys; Fixed-Sampling: n=1000 queries, t=50 tokens (t=75 for Claude 3); Cache-Augmented: Q1=Q2=75
Diversity assumption validated: n-R(n) decays exponentially in response length t; ~200+ tokens suffice for R(n)=n even at low temperature T=0.4 (Fig. 2)
baselines_compared: Tang et al. (2023) - closest prior watermark-detection work, but assumes access to an unwatermarked counterpart model or full model logits (strictly easier setting); this paper does not require either

Watermark scrubbing/spoofing attacks (e.g. Jovanovic et al. 2024) as an alternative detection route - rejected as infeasible for detection because they cannot quantify success and cost far more queries

Comparison is primarily self-referential: each test's p-value under the target watermark family vs under unwatermarked models (temperature varied) and vs models carrying a DIFFERENT watermark family, to demonstrate specificity / absence of false positives
No accuracy/F1 leaderboard baseline; the evaluation metric is median p-value across 100 repetitions (and 5 keys for Red-Green)
